title: Create an array
instruction:
Declare an array with `type[] name = new type[size]` or with an initialiser.

Run this.
successMessage: Arrays are zero-indexed. primes[0] is 2, primes[4] is 11. Length gives the count.

title: Modify elements
instruction:
Assign to an index to change an element.

Run this.
successMessage: Element [2] changed from 78 to 80. Arrays are mutable — you can change elements but not the size.

title: Iterate with for and foreach
instruction:
Use `for` when you need the index, `foreach` when you don't.

Run this.
successMessage: fruits.Length is the upper bound. Loop from 0 to Length-1. Using < Length (not <=) prevents IndexOutOfRangeException.

title: Array.Sort and Array.Reverse
instruction:
Static methods on the `Array` class sort and reverse in place.

Run this.
successMessage: Array.Sort uses IntroSort — O(n log n). Array.Reverse reverses in place. Both modify the original array.

title: Array methods: Min, Max, Sum
instruction:
LINQ adds useful array methods via the `using System.Linq` namespace.

Run this.
successMessage: Min, Max, Sum, Average come from LINQ. They work on any IEnumerable<T>. LINQ will be covered fully in its own chapter.

title: 2D arrays
instruction:
C# supports rectangular 2D arrays with `[,]` syntax.

Run this.
successMessage: matrix[1,2] accesses row 1, column 2 (0-indexed) = 6. GetLength(0) = rows, GetLength(1) = columns.

title: Jagged arrays
instruction:
Jagged arrays are arrays of arrays, each row having different lengths.

Run this.
successMessage: Jagged arrays use [][] syntax. Each row is a separate array. More flexible than rectangular 2D arrays.

title: Array.Copy and spread
instruction:
`Array.Copy` copies elements between arrays.

Run this.
successMessage: Array.Copy makes a shallow copy. Modifying dest[0] does not affect source. For value types like int, this is a full independent copy.
